text: Herein we describe our step-by-step approach for a robotic-assisted laparoscopic superpubic simple prostatectomy. Our patient was a sixty-five-year-old male with history of lower urinary tract symptoms with an international prostate severity score of twenty-one with a quality of life of four, whose symptoms were refractory to medical management and he desired surgical intervention. Truss sizing demonstrated that his prostate volume was eighty grams. We use a standard five port placement with fenestrated bipolar and progress in the left robotic working arms and a scissors in the right with camera in midline and assistant cephalad to the camera and working ports.

The surgeon distends the bladder with 180 cc of normal saline and makes a vertical cystotomy incision to access the bladder. The incision is lengthened to identify the prostate, urethra, and bilateral ureteral orifices. Four stay sutures are placed using 2-0 vicryl on a CT-1 needle, securing the bladder to the abdominal sidewall in superior and inferior positions to facilitate dissection.

The surgeon tensions the four stay sutures to open the cystotomy incision, providing visualization of the prostatic adenoma. A 2-0 Vicryl suture on a CT-1 needle is placed through the prostatic adenoma at the inferior position, avoiding the ureteral orifices, and used as a retraction suture. The surgeon then makes an incision in the prostatic adenoma at the inferior position, carefully avoiding the ureteral orifices, to create a dissection plane for identifying the size and location of the adenoma.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to dissect the fibromuscular aspects of the prostate on the right side, taking care to stay medial to avoid capsular bleeding. The circumferential dissection continues around the prostatic adenoma, moving to the superior portion while avoiding bleeding from the dorsal venous complex. The vicryl stay suture acts as a retraction suture, pulling the prostatic adenoma into the bladder. The apex of the prostatic adenoma is identified along with the catheter, and the remaining attachments are freed before placing the prostatic adenoma in the endocatch bag.

The surgeon advances the urethral mucosa using a 2-0 Vicryl suture with a CT-1 needle after removing the prostatic adenoma. Hemostasis is achieved with electrocautery and hemostatic agents like Floseal. A 22 French three-way Foley catheter is placed, and the inflow is capped to monitor hematuria. Suspension sutures are removed, and the bladder is closed in two layers, starting with the mucosal layer using a running 3-0 V-lock suture with a CV-23 needle.

The surgeon closes the muscle layer of the bladder using a 2-0 V-lock suture on a GS-XXI needle. After closing the bladder, the prostatic adenoma is placed in an endocatch bag. The bladder is then distended to check for watertight integrity, and a JP drain is placed before removing the robotic ports.
